content:
# Philippians Chapter 1 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 194-195 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/PHP_001_page_001.jpg, PHP_001_page_002.jpg

---

**1** From Paul and Timothy, servants of Christ Jesus — to all God's holy people in Philippi, together with your overseers and deacons:

**2** May grace and peace come to you from God our Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

**3** Every time you cross my mind, I thank God for you.

**4** Whenever I pray for all of you, I pray with joy,

**5** because you have been my partners in spreading the good news from the very first day until now.

**6** And I am completely confident of this: the God who started a good work in you will carry it through to completion on the day Christ Jesus returns.

**7** It is only right for me to feel this way about all of you, because you hold a special place in my heart. Whether I am in chains or standing up to defend and confirm the good news, you share in this grace with me.

**8** God himself can testify how deeply I long for every one of you with the tender affection of Christ Jesus.

**9** And this is what I pray: that your love will keep growing richer and richer in real knowledge and every kind of insight,

**10** so that you can discern what truly matters — and then live pure and blameless lives until the day of Christ,

**11** overflowing with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ, bringing glory and praise to God.

**12** Now I want you to know, brothers and sisters, that what has happened to me has actually helped advance the good news.

**13** It has become clear to the entire imperial guard and to everyone else that I am in chains because of Christ.

**14** And most of the brothers and sisters, emboldened by my imprisonment, have found far greater courage to speak God's word fearlessly.

**15** Some, it is true, are preaching Christ out of jealousy and rivalry, but others do it with genuine good will.

**16** Those who act out of love know that I have been placed here to defend the good news.

**17** But the others proclaim Christ out of selfish ambition, not sincerely — thinking they can stir up trouble for me while I am in chains.

**18** But so what? Either way — whether their motives are false or true — Christ is being proclaimed. And that makes me glad. Yes, I will keep on rejoicing,

**19** because I know that through your prayers and the help that comes from the Spirit of Jesus Christ, this will lead to my deliverance.

**20** I eagerly expect and hope that I will have nothing to be ashamed of, but that now as always I will have the courage to exalt Christ in my body — whether by living or by dying.

**21** For to me, living means Christ, and dying is pure gain.

**22** But if I go on living in the body, that means fruitful work for me. So which would I choose? I honestly do not know.

**23** I am pulled in two directions. Part of me longs to depart and be with Christ — which would be far, far better.

**24** But it is more necessary for your sake that I remain in the body.

**25** Since I am convinced of this, I know I will stay and continue with all of you, so that you may grow and find joy in your faith,

**26** and so that through my coming to you again, your pride in Christ Jesus will overflow on my account.

**27** Only this: live your lives in a way that is worthy of the good news of Christ. Then, whether I come and see you or only hear about you from a distance, I will know that you are standing firm with one spirit, fighting side by side with one purpose for the faith of the good news,

**28** and that you are not intimidated in any way by those who oppose you. This is a sign to them of their destruction — but of your salvation, and it comes from God.

**29** For you have been given the privilege, on behalf of Christ, not only of believing in him but also of suffering for him,

**30** since you are going through the same struggle you once saw me face — and as you hear, I am facing still.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "servants" (DOULOI):** The Greek word means "slaves," but I rendered it "servants" because in English "slave" carries associations of dehumanization rooted in chattel slavery. Paul's meaning is voluntary, devoted bondservice — total allegiance to Christ as master. "Servants" conveys the relational dignity Paul intends while preserving the sense of belonging wholly to another.

**Verse 1 — "overseers and deacons" (EPISKOPOIS KAI DIAKONOIS):** I avoided the later ecclesiastical terms "bishops" and "deacons" (which carry heavy institutional freight) in favor of the functional sense visible in the manuscript. "Overseers" captures the role of pastoral supervision; "deacons" is retained as it has become functionally understood in English.

**Verse 6 — "carry it through to completion":** The Greek EPITELESEI conveys active, ongoing work brought to a final point. Rather than the more static "will complete it," I chose language that emphasizes God's sustained effort — he does not merely finish; he carries forward what he began.

**Verse 7 — "you hold a special place in my heart" (ECHEIN ME EN TEI KARDIA):** The Sinaiticus text supports the reading "I have you in my heart." I rendered this as a warmer English expression to match the emotional force Paul clearly intends.

**Verse 8 — "tender affection" (SPLAGCHNOIS):** Literally "guts" or "bowels" — the ancient seat of deep feeling. "Tender affection" captures the visceral depth without the anatomical strangeness.

**Verse 13 — "imperial guard" (PRAITORIOI):** The Sinaiticus reads PRAITORIOI. This refers to the praetorian guard or the praetorium. I chose "imperial guard" as a natural English equivalent that communicates the significance — these are Rome's elite soldiers.

**Verse 18 — "But so what?":** The Greek TI GAR is a sharp rhetorical pivot. A formal "What then?" misses the punch. Paul is genuinely dismissive of his opponents' motives — what matters is the result. "But so what?" captures that energy.

**Verse 21 — "living means Christ, and dying is pure gain":** This is one of the most compressed and powerful statements in Paul's letters. I kept it lean and direct, letting the parallelism do its own work. Adding explanatory words would dilute the force of the original.

**Verse 23 — "far, far better" (POLLOI MALLON KREISSON):** The Sinaiticus text intensifies with a double comparative. Paul is not mildly preferring departure — he is expressing deep longing. The repetition "far, far better" mirrors the intensification in the Greek.

**Verse 27 — "fighting side by side" (SUNATHOUNTES):** An athletic/military metaphor — contending together as teammates in a contest. "Fighting side by side" captures both the communal and combative dimensions.

**Verse 29 — "given the privilege" (ECHARISTHE):** Built on the root CHARIS (grace/gift). Paul frames suffering not as punishment but as something graciously granted. "Given the privilege" preserves this startling theological reversal — suffering for Christ is a gift, not a burden.

**Codex Sinaiticus Observations:** The manuscript shows the characteristic Sinaiticus scriptio continua (continuous script without word breaks). The text of Philippians begins in the third column of page 194 with the header ΠΑΥΛΟΣ ΚΑΙ ΤΙΜΟΘΕΟΣ. Notable Sinaiticus readings include the word order in verse 14, where the manuscript places "of God" (TOU THEOU) with "the word" rather than "of the Lord" — I followed the Sinaiticus reading. The text flows across all four columns of page 195, ending Philippians 1 in the second column before continuing into chapter 2.

— PNEUMA
